title: GPU Bilinear Interpolation
description: Achieve smooth weather gradients by interpolating decoded data values, not pre-colored pixels. Standard GPU texture filtering (GL_LINEAR) interpolates RGBA colors, which produces wrong results for data tiles (e.g., averaging two encoded values gives a nonsensical color). This feature implements manual 4-texel bilinear interpolation in the fragment shader: sample 4 neighbors, decode each to float, THEN interpolate. Also adds separate U/V wind component tiles for correct vector interpolation.

title: Manual 4-texel bilinear interp shader
description: Implement decode-then-interpolate bilinear filtering in the fragment shader. GPU's built-in GL_LINEAR interpolates raw RGBA bytes which gives wrong results for encoded data tiles. Sample 4 neighboring texels with GL_NEAREST, decode each to float, then bilinearly interpolate the decoded values. This eliminates visible pixel stepping in weather gradients.
close_reason: Implemented manual bilinear interpolation in fragment shader. Samples 4 texels with GL_NEAREST, decodes each to float, then bilinearly interpolates with nodata-aware weighting. Uses textureSize() to avoid extra uniform.

title: Wind U/V component data tiles
description: Serve wind as separate U (east-west) and V (north-south) component data tiles instead of pre-computed speed/direction. This is essential for correct wind interpolation — you cannot bilinearly interpolate wind speed+direction (polar coordinates) without artifacts. Interpolating Cartesian U,V components then computing speed=sqrt(U^2+V^2) and direction=atan2(V,U) gives physically correct results.
notes:
## Backend Changes
- New routes: /tiles/{dataset}/{run}/wind_u/data/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
                /tiles/{dataset}/{run}/wind_v/data/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
- Extract U,V components from Zarr (already stored as U10/V10)
- Encode each component independently using same 16-bit encoding
- Value ranges: U,V typically [-50, +50] m/s

## Frontend Changes
- Load 2 tiles per wind cell instead of 1
- Fragment shader: decode U and V separately, interpolate, reconstruct:
  float speed = sqrt(u*u + v*v);
  float direction = atan(v, u);
- Color ramp lookup uses speed (scalar)
- Direction feeds into wind barb/arrow rendering

## Files
- src/weatherman/tiling/router.py (wind_u, wind_v routes)
- src/weatherman/tiling/data_encoder.py (component extraction)
- frontend/src/layers/TileManager.ts (load U+V tile pairs)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.frag.glsl (vector decode)

## Acceptance Criteria
- U,V tiles correctly encode component values
- Reconstructed wind speed matches pre-computed speed tiles +/- 0.1 m/s
- Interpolated wind directions are physically correct near direction discontinuities
- No performance regression from loading 2x tiles (prefetch helps)

title: Phase 5: AIS Derived Products + Maritime Decision Tools
description:
## Phase 5 — AIS Derived Products + Maritime Decision Tools (Weeks 19–24)

**Strategic Goal:** Layer intelligence on top of raw data: weather-adjusted vessel ETAs, port congestion indicators, risk indices, official weather alerts, and passive route weather sampling. Also implement full multi-tenant workspace isolation.

**Why this follows routing:** ETA prediction requires both AIS data (Phase 2) and weather forecasts along projected routes — conceptually similar to routing but simpler (great-circle + speed penalty, not full isochrone). Port congestion uses DuckDB spatial queries against anchorage polygons. These are computed products that add decision-support value without requiring new core infrastructure.

**Key decisions:**
- ETA uses configurable wave_height → speed_penalty lookup (not ML, not yet)
- Port congestion is polygon-count + dwell-time based (simple, interpretable)
- Multi-tenant isolation tested with integration tests verifying no data leakage
- Official alerts start with NWS API (US coverage); other providers later

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Vessel details show weather-adjusted ETA
- Port markers show congestion level; click shows detail
- Alerts render as polygons with severity
- Different tenants see only their scoped data

title: ETA Prediction
description: Compute weather-adjusted ETAs for dry bulk vessels with known destinations. This layers intelligence on raw AIS data — instead of just showing where a vessel IS, we predict when it will ARRIVE, accounting for weather en route. Uses great-circle distance + SOG baseline, then applies wave/wind speed penalties from forecast data.

title: Great-circle distance computation
description: Compute great-circle (haversine) distance from vessel's current position to destination port coordinates. Maintain a reference port geometry lookup (static config: port name → coordinates). Destination comes from AIS destination field — often messy/abbreviated. Consider fuzzy matching to known ports.

title: Base ETA from distance/SOG
description: Simple baseline: ETA = current_time + (distance / current_SOG). Handle edge cases: SOG=0 (vessel at anchor), SOG unrealistically high (data quality issue, cap at max reasonable speed).

title: Weather adjustment (speed penalty)
description: Sample forecast wind and wave along projected route (great-circle path). Apply configurable speed reduction factor: lookup table of wave_height → speed_penalty_pct (e.g., 3m waves → 15% speed reduction). Sum penalties across route segments. This is deliberately simple (not full routing) — good enough for ETA, not optimal-path.

title: Confidence band from weather uncertainty
description: When ensemble data is available, compute ETA range (best/worst case) based on ensemble spread of wave/wind along route. For deterministic models: use ± configurable percentage as proxy.

title: GET /v1/ais/vessels/{mmsi}/eta endpoint
description: API endpoint returning: estimated_arrival timestamp, distance_remaining_nm, base_eta (no weather), adjusted_eta, speed_penalties applied, confidence_band, model_run used. Also include as a field on vessel feature responses.

title: Port Congestion Indicators
description: Compute port congestion levels from AIS data. For each major dry bulk port, count vessels in the anchorage area and compute median dwell time. Classify as low/moderate/high/severe. This gives operators visibility into destination port conditions — critical for voyage planning and charter decisions.

title: Reference anchorage polygon config
description: Define GeoJSON polygons for anchorage areas of major dry bulk ports (start with top 20-30 ports by volume). Static config file, manually curated. Include port name, UNLOCODE, polygon geometry. These polygons define 'at anchor' — vessels inside are counted as congestion.

title: Vessel count within anchorage (DuckDB spatial)
description: For each port/day: count distinct vessels (by MMSI) with positions inside the anchorage polygon. Use DuckDB's ST_Within or ST_Contains with the spatial extension. Output: port_id, date, vessel_count.

title: Median dwell time computation
description: For each port: identify vessels present on consecutive days (same MMSI in anchorage on day N and day N+1, N+2, etc.). Compute median dwell time in days. This measures how long vessels wait — a key congestion signal.

title: Congestion classification
description: Classify each port's congestion level based on configurable thresholds. E.g., low: <5 vessels & <3d dwell, moderate: 5-15 vessels, high: 15-30, severe: >30. Thresholds configurable per port (busy ports have higher baselines).

title: GET /v1/ports/{port_id}/congestion endpoint
description: API endpoint returning: congestion_level, vessel_count, median_dwell_days, trend (improving/worsening/stable based on last 7 days), last_updated. Also return list of vessels currently at anchor (MMSI, vessel_name, days_at_anchor).

title: Risk Indices + Threshold Alert Layers
description: Compute maritime risk indices from weather data and publish as COG layers. These are derived products that transform raw weather fields into maritime-relevant safety indicators. Risk layers are what operators actually care about — not raw wave height, but 'is it safe to transit?'

title: Wave height threshold layer
description: Binary/graduated mask: areas where significant wave height exceeds configurable thresholds (e.g., >4m, >6m, >8m). Published as COG. Used in route risk visualization and alerting.

title: Wave period/steepness proxy
description: Compute wave steepness proxy from height/period ratio. Short-period steep waves are more dangerous than long-period swells of the same height. This adds nuance to the simple height threshold.

title: Comfort index (heuristic)
description: Combined index from wave height, wave period, wind speed. Heuristic formula (weighted combination). Outputs a 0-10 comfort score per grid cell. Useful for passenger/crew comfort assessment. This is deliberately heuristic — not a validated seakeeping model.

title: Port approach risk windows
description: For configured port approaches: compute time windows when conditions are safe for approach (wave height < threshold, wind speed < threshold). Useful for arrival planning. Time-series output per port.

title: Official Alerts Integration
description: Ingest and display official weather alerts/warnings. Start with NWS (US coverage) — clear, well-documented API. Render as polygons with severity metadata. Push alert changes via SSE. Alert coverage varies by region; design for multiple providers.

title: NWS API alert ingestion
description: Poll NWS API for active alerts in maritime zones. Normalize to internal alert schema (provider, severity, headline, description, polygon, valid_time, expires). Configurable poll interval (5 min default). Handle API rate limits.

title: Alert polygon rendering with severity
description: Render alerts as polygons on the map. Color-code by severity (minor/moderate/severe/extreme). Click shows detail panel: headline, description, valid period, source.

title: SSE alert.activated / alert.expired events
description: Emit SSE events when new alerts activate or existing alerts expire. Frontend subscribes and updates alert display in real-time without page refresh.

title: Route Weather Sampling (Passive)
description: Passive weather-along-route sampling (separate from optimization). User draws or selects a route, and EDR trajectory/corridor queries return weather conditions along it. This serves operators who have a fixed route (not optimizing) but want to understand conditions.

title: EDR trajectory endpoint
description: Implement OGC EDR trajectory query: sample weather along a polyline route from Zarr. Return time-series of conditions at each waypoint/segment. Input: GeoJSON LineString + parameter names + time range.

title: EDR corridor endpoint
description: Implement OGC EDR corridor query: sample weather within a buffer zone around a route. Returns spatial statistics (min/max/mean) across the corridor width. Useful for risk band assessment.

title: Timeline charts along track
description: Frontend charts showing weather parameters (wind, wave, pressure) along the route. X-axis: distance along track or waypoint index. Y-axis: parameter value. Color bands for threshold levels.

title: Printable route summary
description: Generate a printable/downloadable route weather summary (PDF or HTML). Includes: route map, weather charts, risk hotspots, ETA estimates. For bridge team distribution. Use server-side rendering (HTML template → PDF via weasyprint or similar).

title: Frontend - Decision Tools
description: Frontend UI components for the Phase 5 decision tools: port congestion overlay, ETA in vessel panel, alert visualization, and model selector.

title: Port congestion overlay
description: Color-coded port markers on the map. Green/yellow/orange/red by congestion level. Click shows congestion detail panel (vessel count, dwell time, trend). Data from congestion API.

title: ETA display in vessel panel
description: Extend the vessel detail panel (from Phase 3) with weather-adjusted ETA: estimated arrival, base vs adjusted comparison, route weather summary, confidence band. Highlight when ETA differs significantly from AIS-reported destination ETA.

title: Alert toggles and legend
description: Alert layer toggle in layer selector. Legend showing alert severity colors. Filter by alert type (marine, coastal, etc.). Active alert count badge.

title: Model selector + comparison view
description: Dropdown to switch between weather models (GFS initially, more later). Side-by-side or overlay comparison mode for multi-model analysis. Shows model metadata (run time, resolution).

title: Multi-Tenant Isolation
description: Implement full multi-tenant workspace isolation. The tenant-ID middleware from Phase 1 is now enforced across all data stores and APIs. Weather data is shared; AIS, routes, alerts config are tenant-scoped. Integration tests verify no data leakage between tenants.

title: Tenant-scoped data tables
description: Add tenant_id column to: saved routes, alert configurations, AIS access grants. Enforce tenant_id filter in all queries touching these tables. Weather data tables remain unscoped (public forecast data).

title: Tenant-scoped AIS vector tile generation
description: If tenants have different AIS data licensing: generate separate MVT tile sets per tenant. If shared: generate shared tiles with access check at endpoint. Support both modes via config.

title: Integration tests for tenant isolation
description: Write integration tests that: create data for tenant A, verify tenant B cannot see it. Cover: routes, AIS queries, alert configs, vector tiles. These tests are the safety net for the most critical security property.

title: Phase 3: Wind Particles + Time Interpolation + AIS Details
description:
## Phase 3 — Wind Particles + Time Interpolation + AIS Details (Weeks 9–12)

**Strategic Goal:** Achieve the Windy.com-style animated wind visualization that is the platform's marquee feature. Smooth time interpolation, GPU-accelerated particle animation, and intelligent prefetching. Also flesh out AIS with vessel details and track playback.

**Why this matters:** This is where the product becomes visually compelling and differentiated. The particle animation is what users will immediately compare to Windy.com. Without smooth interpolation and prefetching, the animation stutters and breaks the illusion. The prefetch manager is critical infrastructure — it ensures data is always ahead of the playback cursor.

**Key technical challenges:**
- WebGL shader pipeline for particle advection + trail fading
- Encoding wind U/V into tile formats (8-bit compat + high-fidelity)
- Web Worker prefetch with priority queue and AbortController
- Maintaining 60fps on mid-range hardware

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Smooth animation at 60fps on mid-range laptop
- Transition between timesteps without visible jumps
- Prefetch eliminates stutter during normal-speed playback
- Vessel click shows track + metadata

title: Wind U/V Tile Encoding
description: Encode wind U and V components into tile formats suitable for GPU particle animation. Two modes: 8-bit packed PNG for broad compatibility, and a high-fidelity option (NumpyTile or binary Float16) for precise interpolation. The encoding quality directly affects particle animation accuracy.

title: 8-bit U/V packed PNG tile path
description: Encode U and V wind components into RGBA channels of a PNG tile. Convention: R=U high byte, G=U low byte (or simpler R=U, G=V with offset+scale). Document the encoding/decoding math. This is the compatibility baseline that works everywhere — any browser with Canvas or WebGL can decode PNG tiles without special handling.

title: High-fidelity option (NumpyTile or binary Float16)
description: Implement at least one high-fidelity wind tile format. NumpyTile via TiTiler returns raw numpy arrays. Alternative: custom binary endpoint serving Float16 U/V pairs. The high-fidelity path gives precise values for professional use where 8-bit quantization artifacts are unacceptable (e.g., light winds near 0 m/s).

title: GPU Particle Engine
description: WebGL-based particle animation engine for wind visualization. This is the marquee visual feature — the 'wow factor' that makes this a Windy.com competitor. Particles are advected by the wind field, with trails rendered using ping-pong framebuffers for the characteristic fading effect.

title: WebGL particle advection shader
description: Implement vertex/fragment shaders for particle advection. Each frame: read wind U/V from texture, update particle position, wrap around globe edges. Use transform feedback or texture-based state. Start with 50k-100k particles. Key perf consideration: texture lookups in the shader are the bottleneck. Use LINEAR filtering for smooth interpolation between texels.

title: Ping-pong framebuffer trail fading
description: Implement trail rendering: render particles to FBO A, blend FBO A onto FBO B with alpha fade, swap. This creates the characteristic wind trail visualization. Fade rate is configurable (faster = shorter trails). This is a well-known technique; the key is getting the alpha blend right so trails look natural and don't accumulate artifacts over time.

title: Adaptive particle count
description: Detect device GPU capability (WebGL renderer string, benchmark frame time). Reduce particle count on weaker devices, increase on powerful ones. Target: 60fps on mid-range 2024 laptop. Fallback: static wind barb overlay if WebGL2 unavailable. This ensures the particle engine degrades gracefully across the full range of client hardware.

title: Time Interpolation
description: Smooth interpolation between discrete forecast timesteps (typically 3h or 6h apart). Without this, the animation 'jumps' every timestep change — destroying the smooth Windy experience. Scalars interpolate linearly; vectors interpolate in vector space; particles use the interpolated field for advection.

title: Scalar linear interpolation
description: For scalar fields (temperature, pressure, wave height): linearly interpolate between the two bracketing timesteps based on animation clock position. Decode tile data, interpolate per-pixel, re-encode for rendering. This is the foundation interpolation method that all other interpolation builds on.

title: Vector (U/V) interpolation
description: For wind U/V: interpolate U and V independently (linear in vector space). This preserves wind direction continuity. Do NOT interpolate speed+direction (causes artifacts at direction reversals near 0/360 degrees). Uses the same framework as scalar interpolation but applied to each vector component separately.

title: Particle advection with interpolated field
description: Feed the interpolated U/V field to the particle advection shader. The interpolation happens on each animation frame, so the wind field smoothly evolves and particles follow the evolving flow. This connects the interpolation pipeline to the GPU particle engine for seamless animated wind visualization.

title: Client-Side Prefetch Manager
description: Web Worker-based predictive tile prefetching that ensures data is always available ahead of the playback cursor. This is critical infrastructure — without it, every timestep transition causes a network fetch delay and visible stutter. The prefetch manager handles both timeline (next timesteps) and spatial (pan direction) prefetching.

title: Web Worker prefetch architecture
description: Set up dedicated Web Worker for tile fetching. Main thread sends viewport bounds + current time; worker manages fetch queue. Communication via postMessage. Worker owns the fetch lifecycle (including AbortController for cancellation). Keeping fetches in a worker prevents main thread jank from network callbacks.

title: Timeline prefetch (next 2-3 timesteps)
description: During playback: prefetch tiles for next 2-3 timesteps at current viewport bounds. When paused/scrubbing: prefetch +/-1 timestep from scrub position. This ensures interpolation always has both bracketing timesteps available in cache, preventing stalls during smooth animation playback.

title: Pan prefetch (direction-of-movement tiles)
description: On pan gesture: compute movement velocity vector, prefetch one tile ring beyond visible viewport in the direction of movement at current timestep. This prevents blank tiles appearing at viewport edges during pan. Lower priority than timeline prefetch since panning is less frequent than time advancement.

title: Priority queue + AbortController
description: Implement priority queue in worker: current_viewport_current_time > current_viewport_next_time > adjacent_viewport_current_time. Use AbortController to cancel stale requests when viewport/time changes significantly. Max concurrent fetches: 6 (configurable). This prevents wasted bandwidth on tiles that are no longer needed.

title: Bounded LRU tile cache (~200MB)
description: In-memory LRU cache in the worker for fetched tiles. Max size ~200MB (configurable). Main thread requests tiles from worker cache before falling back to network. Track cache hit rate as a metric. Eviction policy: least-recently-used with size-aware accounting (different tile formats have different sizes).

title: Frontend - Timeline & Animation
description: Timeline control UI for playing, pausing, scrubbing, and adjusting speed of the forecast animation. The animation loop coordinates tile loading, interpolation, and rendering at 60fps.

title: Timeline control UI
description: Implement timeline bar with: play/pause button, speed selector (0.5x/1x/2x/4x), scrub slider showing forecast hours, current time display. Keyboard shortcuts (space=play/pause, left/right=step). The timeline is the primary user interaction point for temporal navigation of forecast data.

title: Smooth animation loop at 60fps
description: requestAnimationFrame loop that: advances animation clock, requests interpolated data for current time, renders frame. Decouple animation clock from wall clock (configurable speed). Drop frames gracefully if rendering can't keep up. The animation loop is the central coordinator between timeline UI, data fetching, interpolation, and rendering.

title: AIS Vessel Details
description: Enhanced AIS interaction: vessel detail panel with full metadata, track playback visualization, and bbox query endpoint. This builds on Phase 2's directional vessel arrows to provide actionable vessel information.

title: Vessel detail panel
description: On vessel click: show side panel with MMSI, IMO, vessel name, SOG/COG, heading, destination, draught, timestamp, vessel type. Styled consistently with other panels. This panel will grow in Phase 5 to include ETA and track, so design with extensibility in mind — use a component architecture that supports adding new data sections.

title: Track playback visualization
description: Fetch vessel track (time-ordered positions from Phase 2 track table) and render as a line on the map. Color-code by SOG or time. Animate playback (dot moving along track). Use MapLibre line layer with data-driven styling. The track visualization connects the vessel's historical positions into a meaningful narrative.

title: AIS bbox query endpoint
description: Implement GET /v1/ais/vessels?bbox=minlon,minlat,maxlon,maxlat&date=YYYY-MM-DD. DuckDB spatial query with ST_Within or bbox filter. Return GeoJSON FeatureCollection. Paginate if needed. This enables the frontend to fetch vessel details for the current viewport on demand rather than loading all vessels globally.

title: Data-Encoded Tile Pipeline
description: The FOUNDATIONAL change for GPU-first rendering. Currently TiTiler generates server-side-colorized PNG tiles — the browser receives pretty pictures but has no access to the underlying data values. This feature adds a parallel data tile endpoint that encodes float32 weather values into RGBA PNG channels (16-bit precision), plus the WebGL infrastructure to decode and colorize them on the GPU. This unlocks: bilinear interpolation between decoded values (not pre-colored pixels), temporal blending between timesteps, and dynamic color ramp switching without re-fetching tiles.
notes:
## Architecture
- Server: New /tiles/{dataset}/{run}/{field}/data/{z}/{x}/{y}.png route
- Encoding: float32 → 16-bit unsigned int → R,G channels (little-endian)
- B channel: reserved for flags (nodata=0xFF, valid=0x00)
- A channel: always 0xFF (opaque) to prevent PNG pre-multiplication issues
- Client: MapLibre CustomLayerInterface with WebGL2 shaders
- Decode in fragment shader: value = (R + G*256) / 65535 * (max-min) + min

## Files
- src/weatherman/tiling/router.py (new endpoint)
- src/weatherman/tiling/data_encoder.py (new module)
- frontend/src/layers/WeatherGLLayer.ts (new)
- frontend/src/layers/TileManager.ts (new)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.vert.glsl (new)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.frag.glsl (new)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Data tiles produce pixel-perfect round-trip: encode → decode = original value ± 0.1%
- WebGL layer renders identically to current TiTiler colored tiles
- GPU memory stays under 256MB for typical viewport
- Feature flag allows toggling between old and new pipeline
close_reason: All 5 sub-tasks completed: backend data tile endpoint, WebGL scaffold, tile manager, fragment shader decode+colorize, and feature flag integration. Data-encoded tile pipeline is fully functional behind VITE_USE_WEBGL_WEATHER flag.

title: Backend data tile endpoint
description: Create a new TiTiler route that serves weather data as encoded RGBA PNGs instead of colorized images. The endpoint encodes float32 weather values into 16-bit unsigned integers spread across R,G channels, with B channel for flags (nodata) and A=0xFF. This is the server-side half of the GPU rendering pipeline — without it, the WebGL shaders have no data to decode.
notes:
## Endpoint Design
Route: GET /tiles/{dataset}/{run}/{field}/data/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
- Same path structure as existing colored tiles, with /data/ segment added
- Returns 256x256 PNG with encoded values
- Response headers must include X-Value-Range: min,max for client-side decode

## Encoding Algorithm
1. Read float32 values from Zarr/COG via TiTiler
2. Normalize to [0, 1] range using field-specific min/max from colormaps.py
3. Scale to [0, 65535] unsigned 16-bit integer
4. R = value & 0xFF (low byte), G = (value >> 8) & 0xFF (high byte)
5. B = 0x00 for valid data, 0xFF for nodata
6. A = 0xFF always (prevents PNG pre-multiplication artifacts)

## Files to Modify/Create
- src/weatherman/tiling/router.py — add /data/ route
- src/weatherman/tiling/data_encoder.py — new module for float->RGBA encoding
- src/weatherman/tiling/colormaps.py — expose value ranges per field

## Acceptance Criteria
- Round-trip test: encode -> decode matches original +/- 0.1%
- Nodata pixels correctly flagged
- Response time within 2x of existing colored tile endpoint
- Works for all existing weather fields (wind, pressure, temperature, waves)
close_reason: Implemented data tile endpoint with float-to-RGBA encoding. All acceptance criteria met: round-trip accuracy within 0.1%, nodata correctly flagged, works for all weather fields (temperature, wind_speed, precipitation). 49 tests passing.

title: WebGL custom layer scaffold
description: Implement MapLibre's CustomLayerInterface for weather rendering. This is the WebGL entry point — it owns the GL context lifecycle, compiles shaders, manages the render loop integration with MapLibre's camera, and provides the foundation that TileManager and shaders plug into.
notes:
## CustomLayerInterface Contract
Must implement:
- onAdd(map, gl): Initialize shaders, buffers, textures
- render(gl, matrix): Called each frame by MapLibre
- onRemove(map, gl): Cleanup all GL resources

## Key Implementation Details
- Use WebGL2 (gl.getContext('webgl2')) for texture array support
- Compile vertex + fragment shaders with error reporting
- Create fullscreen quad (2 triangles) for fragment shader rendering
- Pass MapLibre's projection matrix as uniform for geo-correct rendering
- Handle map resize events (update viewport, recreate framebuffers if needed)

## Shader Compilation Helper
- Load GLSL from imported strings (vite raw import)
- Compile with error checking + line-number-annotated error messages
- Cache compiled programs

## Files
- frontend/src/layers/WeatherGLLayer.ts (new)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.vert.glsl (new — passthrough vertex shader)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.frag.glsl (new — placeholder)
- frontend/src/layers/gl-utils.ts (new — shader compile, buffer helpers)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Layer adds to MapLibre without errors
- Renders a solid color (placeholder) that tracks map viewport
- Clean GL resource disposal on layer remove
- No WebGL errors in console
close_reason: Implemented WeatherGLLayer (CustomLayerInterface) with shader compilation, fullscreen quad geometry, GL resource lifecycle, and placeholder rendering. TypeScript and Vite build both pass clean.

title: WebGL tile manager
description: Manage loading data tile PNGs and uploading them as WebGL textures. Handles tile URL construction, fetch lifecycle, Image->texture upload, LRU eviction, and provides the shader with the correct texture for each visible map tile. This bridges the network layer and the GPU.
notes:
## Responsibilities
1. Compute visible tile coordinates from MapLibre viewport
2. Construct data tile URLs: /tiles/{dataset}/{run}/{field}/data/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
3. Fetch tiles as Image elements (browser handles PNG decode)
4. Upload to WebGL textures via gl.texImage2D
5. LRU cache: keep ~128 textures, evict oldest unused
6. Track loading state: pending/loaded/error per tile
7. Provide texture lookup: getTileTexture(z, x, y) -> WebGLTexture | null

## Key Design Decisions
- Use Image element loading (not fetch+arrayBuffer) for browser-native PNG decode
- GL_NEAREST sampling (not GL_LINEAR) — we do manual bilinear in shader
- RGBA format, UNSIGNED_BYTE type for the encoded data tiles
- Texture atlas vs individual textures: start with individual, optimize later if needed

## Files
- frontend/src/layers/TileManager.ts (new)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tiles load and display as map pans/zooms
- No texture leaks (GPU memory stable over time)
- Loading states reported for UI loading indicators
- Handles 404s and network errors gracefully
close_reason: TileManager.ts implemented with Image-based fetch, WebGL texture upload, LRU eviction (128 max), loading state tracking, visible tile computation, and graceful error handling. TypeScript + Vite build pass clean.

title: Fragment shader decode + colorize
description: The core rendering shader. Decodes 16-bit encoded values from data tile textures, applies a color ramp lookup to map values to colors, and handles nodata pixels (transparent). This replaces server-side colorization with GPU colorization, enabling real-time color ramp changes and data-aware interpolation.
close_reason: Fragment shader decode + colorize implemented. Shader decodes 16-bit R,G data tiles, color ramp 1D LUT textures, WeatherGLLayer updated with texture unit management, colormaps JSON endpoint added. 54 tests pass, TS + Vite build clean.

title: Feature flag + integration
description: Wire the WebGL weather layer into the existing useWeatherLayer hook with a VITE_USE_WEBGL_WEATHER feature flag. When enabled, swap from MapLibre raster source/layer to the custom WebGL layer. When disabled, existing behavior unchanged. This allows gradual rollout and A/B testing.
notes:
## Implementation
- Add VITE_USE_WEBGL_WEATHER to .env and vite config
- In useWeatherLayer.ts: check flag, conditionally create WeatherGLLayer vs raster layer
- WeatherGLLayer needs same inputs: dataset, run, field, opacity, timestep
- Ensure layer add/remove lifecycle matches existing raster layer behavior
- Forward opacity control to WebGL uniform

## Files
- frontend/src/hooks/useWeatherLayer.ts (conditional logic)
- frontend/src/components/MapView.tsx (pass feature flag context)
- frontend/.env.example (document new env var)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Flag=false: zero behavioral change from current code
- Flag=true: WebGL layer renders identically to raster layer
- Toggling flag requires only page refresh, not code change
- No import of WebGL code when flag is false (dynamic import)
close_reason: Feature flag VITE_USE_WEBGL_WEATHER wired into useWeatherLayer with conditional delegation to useWebGLWeatherLayer hook. WeatherGLLayer refactored for multi-tile rendering with map projection. Fresh-eyes caught render-before-config bug, fixed.

title: Integration tests for composed app
description:
Create tests/test_app.py with TestClient-based integration tests that verify the composed FastAPI app works end-to-end.

WHY: The individual modules (health, tiling, tenancy, observability) each have their own unit tests. But the app factory introduces new concerns: correct middleware ordering, lifespan initialization, router mounting, and catalog/manifest route correctness. Integration tests catch wiring bugs that unit tests miss — e.g., a router not being mounted, middleware in wrong order, or lifespan not initializing the tile service.

TEST SETUP PATTERN:
  @pytest.fixture
  def app(tmp_path):
      # Create test data directory with catalog and manifest
      models_dir = tmp_path / "models" / "gfs"
      models_dir.mkdir(parents=True)

      # Write a valid catalog.json
      catalog = RunCatalog.new("gfs")
      catalog.publish_run(RunID("20260306T00Z"), layout=StorageLayout("gfs"))
      catalog.save(models_dir / "catalog.json")

      # Write a valid manifest.json at the expected path
      manifest_dir = tmp_path / "models" / "gfs" / "runs" / "20260306T00Z" / "ui"
      manifest_dir.mkdir(parents=True)
      manifest = build_manifest(ManifestConfig(...))
      (manifest_dir / "manifest.json").write_text(manifest.to_json())

      return create_app(data_dir=str(tmp_path), titiler_base_url="http://localhost:9999")

  @pytest.fixture
  def client(app):
      return TestClient(app)

TEST CASES:

1. test_health_live — GET /health/live → 200 with {"status": "alive"}
   Verifies health router is mounted and liveness probe works independently of dependencies.

2. test_health_ready_no_deps — GET /health/ready → 200 when health checks can't reach TiTiler
   In test environment TiTiler isn't running. The readiness probe should still work (returns degraded or not_ready, but doesn't crash). Verifies graceful degradation.

3. test_metrics_endpoint — GET /metrics → 200 with text/plain content containing "http_request_duration_seconds"
   Verifies Prometheus middleware is wired and metrics endpoint is mounted.

4. test_catalog_endpoint — GET /api/catalog/gfs → 200 with correct JSON shape
   Verifies: catalog route mounted, ObjectStore reads from data_dir, RunCatalog deserialization, response matches frontend expectations (model, current_run_id, runs[]).

5. test_catalog_not_found — GET /api/catalog/nonexistent → 404
   Verifies error handling when model catalog doesn't exist.

6. test_manifest_endpoint — GET /api/manifest/gfs/20260306T00Z → 200 with correct JSON shape
   Verifies: manifest route mounted, path construction via StorageLayout, UIManifest deserialization, response includes schema_version, layers[], forecast_hours[].

7. test_manifest_invalid_run_id — GET /api/manifest/gfs/INVALID → 400
   Verifies RunID validation propagates as 400 Bad Request.

8. test_cors_preflight — OPTIONS /api/catalog/gfs with Origin header → includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin
   Verifies CORS middleware is properly positioned (must be outermost to handle preflight before other middleware).

9. test_tenant_middleware_sets_default — GET /health/live → response completes and request.state.tenant_id was set
   Verifies TenantMiddleware runs on every request (check via structlog context or a custom test middleware).

POTENTIAL ISSUES:
- Prometheus global registry: calling create_app() multiple times in tests may cause metric re-registration errors. Solution: use prometheus_client.REGISTRY.unregister() in fixture teardown, or use CollectorRegistry(auto_describe=True) in test mode.
- OTel tracing: setup_tracing() in lifespan may conflict between test runs. The existing setup_tracing has idempotency handling — verify this works with TestClient's lifespan management.

EXISTING TEST PATTERNS TO FOLLOW: See tests/test_health.py for health check test patterns, tests/test_tenancy.py for middleware testing patterns.

title: CLI entrypoint (__main__.py)
description:
Create src/weatherman/__main__.py so developers can start the server with uv run python -m weatherman.

WHY: Python's -m flag runs __main__.py from a package. This is the standard way to make a package directly executable. For Weatherman, this means a single command starts the full API server — no need to remember uvicorn CLI arguments or module paths.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:
  import uvicorn
  from weatherman.app import create_app

  app = create_app()

  if __name__ == "__main__":
      uvicorn.run("weatherman.__main__:app", host="0.0.0.0", port=8000, reload=True, log_level="info")

KEY DESIGN DECISIONS:
- Module-level app = create_app() — enables both python -m weatherman AND uvicorn weatherman.__main__:app
- String import "weatherman.__main__:app" (not the object) — required for uvicorn's --reload mode to work (it needs to re-import the module on changes)
- reload=True for dev — watches src/ for changes and restarts automatically
- host="0.0.0.0" — listen on all interfaces (needed for Docker, remote dev)
- port=8000 — matches frontend's Vite proxy config and .env.example

USAGE:
  uv run python -m weatherman                    # dev with auto-reload
  uv run uvicorn weatherman.__main__:app         # production (no reload)

NOTES:
- The __main__.py is thin by design — all composition logic lives in app.py's create_app()
- Environment variables (WEATHERMAN_DATA_DIR, TITILER_BASE_URL, etc.) are read by create_app(), not __main__.py
- For production deployment, the Helm charts will call uvicorn directly with appropriate worker count

title: Flicker-Free Time Stepping
description: Eliminate the visible flash/gap when switching between forecast timesteps. Two approaches: (1) QUICK WIN cross-fade using two MapLibre raster layers with opacity animation — works with current colored tiles, no WebGL needed. (2) ADVANCED temporal interpolation in the WebGL shader, blending between T0 and T1 data tiles with a u_temporalMix uniform for buttery-smooth playback.
notes:
## Two-Phase Approach
Phase A (Quick Win - no WebGL dependency):
- Maintain two raster source/layer pairs: 'weather-a' and 'weather-b'
- On timestep change: load new tiles into inactive layer, cross-fade using requestAnimationFrame
- Opacity transition: active 1→0, incoming 0→1 over ~300ms
- Works with existing colored PNG tiles

Phase B (WebGL - depends on Feature 1):
- Bind T0 and T1 data textures to separate texture units
- Add u_temporalMix uniform (0.0 = T0, 1.0 = T1)
- Fragment shader: mix(decode(T0), decode(T1), u_temporalMix) then colorize
- Driven by animation loop synced to timeline slider

## Files
- frontend/src/hooks/useWeatherLayer.ts (cross-fade logic)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/weather.frag.glsl (temporal blend)

## Acceptance Criteria
- No visible flash/gap during timestep transitions
- Cross-fade works at all playback speeds
- Temporal interpolation produces physically plausible intermediate values
close_reason: Both children complete: cross-fade raster layers (wx-3s5.1) and temporal interpolation in shader (wx-3s5.2). All acceptance criteria met.

title: Cross-fade raster layers
description: QUICK WIN. Eliminate the flash when switching forecast timesteps by cross-fading between two raster layers. Maintain two MapLibre raster source/layer pairs — when the timestep changes, load new tiles into the inactive layer and animate opacity (active 1→0, incoming 0→1) using requestAnimationFrame. No WebGL dependency — works with existing colored tiles.
notes:
## Implementation
- Maintain state: { activeLayer: 'a' | 'b', sources: { a: RasterSource, b: RasterSource } }
- On timestep change:
  1. Update inactive source URL to new timestep tiles
  2. Wait for tiles to load (map.on('sourcedata'))
  3. Start cross-fade animation:
     - requestAnimationFrame loop
     - Duration: 300ms
     - Ease: cubic-bezier for smooth feel
     - Set paint properties: raster-opacity on both layers
  4. Swap active/inactive

## Edge Cases
- Rapid timestep changes: cancel in-flight cross-fade, jump to new target
- Initial load: no cross-fade, just show
- Network error on new tiles: stay on current, don't fade to blank

## Files
- frontend/src/hooks/useWeatherLayer.ts (dual-layer management + animation)

## Acceptance Criteria
- No visible flash/gap during normal playback
- Handles rapid scrubbing without visual artifacts
- Works at 1x, 2x, 4x playback speeds
- Graceful degradation on slow connections
close_reason: Implemented cross-fade animation between raster layers using requestAnimationFrame with cubic ease-out over 300ms

title: Temporal interpolation in shader
description: ADVANCED. Blend between two timestep data tiles in the fragment shader for smooth temporal interpolation. Bind T0 and T1 data textures simultaneously, decode both, mix based on a u_temporalMix uniform (0.0=T0, 1.0=T1), then colorize the interpolated value. This produces physically plausible intermediate frames.
close_reason: Implemented temporal interpolation: dual TileManager instances, shader blends decoded T0/T1 values via u_temporalMix uniform, per-tile fallback to T0 when T1 tiles not loaded

title: Phase 2: Canonical Zarr + EDR + AIS Ingest
description:
## Phase 2 — Canonical Zarr + EDR + AIS Ingest (Weeks 5–8)

**Strategic Goal:** Build the canonical Zarr data store per run (enabling point sampling and future routing), expose the first OGC API – EDR endpoint, expand weather layers, and bring AIS data onto the map for the first time.

**Why this phase order:** Zarr is prerequisite for EDR, routing (Phase 4), and derived products (Phase 5). AIS is moved earlier (from original later phases) because daily Parquet ingest is low-risk and gives immediate visible value — vessel dots on the map transform the product from a generic weather viewer into a maritime tool.

**Key decisions:**
- Zarr is THE canonical dataset (COGs are map-optimized projections of it)
- DuckDB with spatial extension for AIS (embedded, no separate server)
- SSE push channel eliminates polling for new data
- AIS filtered to dry bulk only (scope constraint)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Click-on-map shows point time-series quickly (EDR position)
- No partial runs visible
- Dry bulk vessel dots appear on map from daily Parquet
- New runs auto-appear in UI without page refresh (SSE)

Core identifiers: imommsi (string, composite '{imo}-{mmsi}'), mmsi (int), lrimoshipno (string), movementid (string, row-level unique ID).

Vessel info: shipname (string), shiptype (string, e.g. 'Cargo'), vessel_class (string, e.g. 'Capesize', 'Valemax'), dwt (int), callsign (string), beam (double), length (double).

Position/movement: movementdatetime (timestamp), movement_date (date, partition key), latitude (double), longitude (double), speed (double, SOG), heading (double), draught (double), max_draught (double), movestatus (string, nav status).

Destination: destination (string), destinationtidied (string, cleaned), eta (timestamp).

4. Tile manager + fragment shader (Day 7-8, parallel)
5. Feature flag integration (Day 9)
6. Bilinear interp + wind U/V + temporal shader (Day 10-11)
7. Particle foundation (Day 12-19)

## Key Files
- frontend/src/hooks/useWeatherLayer.ts
- frontend/src/utils/basemap-style.ts
- src/weatherman/tiling/router.py
- src/weatherman/tiling/colormaps.py
- frontend/src/components/MapView.tsx
- NEW: frontend/src/layers/WeatherGLLayer.ts

## Cross-Epic Dependencies
This epic blocks wx-0pg.2 (GPU Particle Engine) and wx-0pg.3 (Time Interpolation)

title: Add browser-level tests for forecast controls and weather inspector
description: The new forecast slider/player and click-to-query weather inspector are covered by TypeScript/build checks but not by browser-level interaction tests. Add end-to-end coverage for hour changes, inspector fetch/render, and AIS bootstrap behavior.

title: Vite dev proxy configuration
close_reason: Closed

title: Phase 6: Advanced Modeling + Offline Mode
description:
## Phase 6 — Advanced Modeling + Offline Mode (Weeks 25–32)

**Strategic Goal:** Multi-model blending, ensemble uncertainty visualization, optional AI forecast layers, voyage package generation for offline/vessel-side use, schema versioning enforcement, and full provenance query capability.

**Why this is last:** These are high-value but high-complexity features that build on everything before them. Multi-model blending requires the single-model pipeline to be rock-solid first. Offline mode requires tiles, routing, EDR, and AIS to all work — then packages them for disconnected use. Ensemble uncertainty requires ensemble model support (not just deterministic GFS).

**Key decisions:**
- MosaicJSON for multi-model virtual mosaics (config-driven, not code changes)
- Voyage packages target < 50 MB for transoceanic routes (corridor-scoped tiles at limited zoom)
- Offline viewer is static HTML + MapLibre + PMTiles (no server dependency)
- AI forecast layers are LABELED as AI, never presented as ground truth
- Schema versioning enforced in CI (breaking changes caught before deploy)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Multi-model comparisons understandable in UI
- Voyage package < 50 MB for transoceanic route
- Offline viewer works with no network
- Provenance endpoint returns full audit trail for any run

title: Multi-Model Blending
description: Support multiple weather models (GFS + ICON + ECMWF) with spatial blending and virtual mosaics. MosaicJSON + TiTiler enables config-driven multi-model composition without code changes. Regional high-res models near coasts, global models offshore, with configurable blending zones at seams.

title: MosaicJSON configuration
description: Define MosaicJSON virtual mosaics that combine multiple model COGs. TiTiler reads MosaicJSON and serves blended tiles. Configuration per layer: which models to include, priority order, spatial bounds.

title: Spatial blending (regional + global)
description: Implement blending at model boundaries: weighted average in overlap zones (configurable blend width). Regional high-res model gets priority near coasts; global model fills offshore gaps. Notes: Seam artifacts are the main risk — visual QA is essential.

title: Seam handling and weighting
description: Advanced seam strategies: distance-weighted blending, elevation-aware blending (different treatment at coastlines), configurable per-layer blend functions. Expose blend weights as a debug layer.

title: Ensemble Uncertainty Visualization
description: When ensemble model data is available (e.g., GEFS), compute and visualize uncertainty. Spread fields, probability-of-threshold layers, and ensemble plumes in charts. This communicates forecast confidence to users — critical for risk-aware decision making.

title: Spread fields
description: Compute ensemble spread (standard deviation across members) for key variables (wind speed, wave height). Publish as additional COG layers. Low spread = high confidence, high spread = uncertainty. Display as a semi-transparent overlay.

title: Probability-of-threshold layers
description: For each variable and configurable threshold (e.g., wave_height > 4m): compute fraction of ensemble members exceeding threshold. Publish as probability layer (0-100%). Display as a heatmap overlay.

title: Ensemble plume in point/route charts
description: In point forecast and route weather charts: show all ensemble members as a spaghetti plot (plume). Highlight median/mean and spread envelope. This gives users a visual feel for forecast uncertainty over time.

title: AI Forecast Model Layers (Optional)
description: Ingest and display NOAA AI forecast models (AIGFS, AIGEFS, HGEFS). These are CLEARLY LABELED as AI-generated forecasts, never presented as ground truth. They offer a 'what does the AI model think?' perspective alongside traditional NWP models. Optional feature — not required for production.

title: AIGFS/AIGEFS/HGEFS ingestion pipeline
description: Add ingestion jobs for NOAA AI forecast models (available via AWS NODD). Same pipeline as GFS but with model-specific GRIB2/Zarr handling. Separate model namespace in storage layout.

title: AI forecast labeling
description: In the frontend: AI forecast layers are visually distinct (different palette, 'AI' badge, tooltip explaining these are machine learning forecasts). Never show AI forecasts as the default model. Always require explicit user opt-in.

title: Voyage Package Generation
description: Generate self-contained voyage packages for offline/vessel-side use. A voyage package bundles corridor-scoped tiles (PMTiles), route forecast data, AIS snapshot, and port congestion data into a single downloadable archive. Ships with limited satellite bandwidth download this once and run it locally. Target: < 50 MB for a transoceanic voyage.

title: POST /v1/voyage-packages/generate endpoint
description: API endpoint: input is route (waypoints or route_id), buffer radius (nm), zoom range, layer selection. Returns a job ID (async generation). On completion: signed download URL. Include job status polling endpoint.

title: Corridor-scoped tile extraction → PMTiles
description: Extract tiles from COGs for the route corridor (route ± buffer radius) at specified zoom range. Write as PMTiles file. Also generate a basemap PMTiles for the corridor region. Notes: PMTiles is a single-file tile archive format — perfect for offline use. Use go-pmtiles or pmtiles CLI.

title: EDR sample extraction for route
description: Pre-compute trajectory and corridor EDR samples for the route. Store as static JSON in the package. Includes weather conditions at each waypoint and risk assessments.

title: AIS snapshot for corridor region
description: Extract AIS positions within the corridor buffer. Generate a GeoJSON snapshot (not full MVT — too heavy for offline). Include vessel names, SOG/COG.

title: Package assembly + signed URL
description: Assemble all components into a tar.gz or zip. Generate a manifest.json (metadata, model run info, generation time, expiry timestamp). Create a time-limited signed URL for download. Notes: Expiry in manifest is critical — the offline viewer shows a warning if the data is too old.

title: Offline Vessel-Side Viewer
description: A minimal offline viewer that reads voyage packages from local filesystem. Static HTML + MapLibre + PMTiles — no server needed. Can be packaged as an Electron app or served from a tiny local HTTP server. This is the vessel-side product — what bridge officers see on the ship.

title: Static HTML + MapLibre + PMTiles viewer
description: Build a standalone HTML application: MapLibre GL JS reading basemap + weather tiles from local PMTiles files via the PMTiles protocol adapter. No external network requests. Notes: MapLibre has built-in PMTiles protocol support. The viewer is a single index.html + JS bundle + voyage package directory.

title: Route and forecast rendering from static JSON
description: Render the pre-computed route on the map. Display weather charts from static JSON data. Show risk hotspots, ETA, port congestion. All data comes from the voyage package — no API calls.

title: Optional Electron packaging
description: Package the viewer as an Electron desktop app for easy installation on ship PCs. Auto-detect voyage packages in a configured directory. Notes: This is nice-to-have; the static HTML version can also be served via Python http.server or nginx.

title: Schema Versioning in CI
description: Enforce JSON schema validation in CI for all contracts: UI manifest, catalog index, STAC items, run registry records, AIS normalized rows. Catch breaking schema changes before they reach production. This is the long-term maintainability safeguard.

title: JSON schema validation for contracts
description: Define JSON Schema files for: manifest.json, catalog.json, STAC items, route results, AIS normalized schema. Validate all generated artifacts against schemas in CI.

title: Breaking change detection in CI
description: Compare schema changes against previous version. Minor changes (adding optional fields) pass. Breaking changes (removing fields, changing types, renaming) fail CI and require explicit version bump.

title: Full Provenance Query
description: Expose a provenance query API that returns the full dependency graph for any published run. This enables audit queries: 'what data was the platform showing for location X at time T?' Reads from STAC items' processing extension fields.

title: GET /v1/provenance/{model}/{run_id} endpoint
description: API endpoint returning: source URIs, processing version, QC results, timestamps, Herbie source selection, any derived product lineage. Returns a graph structure (run → inputs → processing steps → outputs).

title: Full dependency graph traversal
description: If a derived product references multiple source runs (e.g., blended model), traverse the full dependency graph. Return all ancestor runs and their provenance. Notes: This is important for auditability in regulated maritime contexts.

title: Phase 4: Production Hardening + Routing Engine
description:
## Phase 4 — Production Hardening + Routing Engine (Weeks 13–18)

**Strategic Goal:** Make the platform production-ready with full workflow orchestration (Argo), blocking QC gates, authentication, graceful degradation, and — crucially — the weather routing optimization engine that transforms the platform from visualization into decision support.

**Why routing is here (not later):** The routing engine is the platform's core differentiator for maritime users. It requires Zarr (Phase 2) and benefits from the multi-layer expansion, but doesn't need AIS-derived products. Shipping it alongside production hardening means the first production deployment has the flagship feature.

**Key decisions:**
- Argo Workflows for DAG orchestration (not just cron scripts)
- QC gate MUST block publish on failure (no manual override without explicit ops action)
- Graceful degradation: stale data > no data, always
- Isochrone-based routing solver (not A* on a graph — continuous wavefront expansion)
- OIDC/JWT for auth, RBAC for authorization

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Pipeline failures visible and actionable
- Rollbacks are a metadata flip (not data deletion)
- Degraded mode works (kill TiTiler → pre-rendered tiles served, banner shown)
- Route optimization returns result < 8s p95
- User can plan route, see weather along track, identify risk windows

title: Argo DAG Workflows
description: Replace ad-hoc pipeline scripts with Argo Workflows DAG definitions. The DAG encodes the full pipeline: acquire → validate → extract → build Zarr → QC → build COGs → build derived products → build STAC → publish. Argo provides retry, failure handling, artifact passing, and observability out of the box. This is the operational backbone of the platform.

title: Argo Workflow DAG definition
description: Define the full pipeline as an Argo DAG template. Each step is a container with defined inputs/outputs. Artifact passing between steps via S3. Parameterize for model name and run ID. Steps: acquire → validate → extract → build Zarr → QC → build COGs → build derived products → build STAC → publish.

title: Per-step retry + failure handling
description: Configure retry policies per step (e.g., ingestion: 3 retries with backoff; processing: 2 retries). On terminal failure: mark run as failed, alert, keep staging artifacts for debugging. Never leave a run in an ambiguous state. Each step must have explicit success/failure transitions.

title: Backfill/reprocess tooling
description: CLI/API to trigger reprocessing of a specific historical run (e.g., after a pipeline bug fix). Reprocess generates new artifacts with a new processing_version, publishes alongside (or replacing) the original. Idempotency key prevents duplicates. Supports selective step re-execution.

title: QC Gate (Blocking)
description: Upgrade the Phase 2 QC checks from advisory (log warnings) to blocking (prevent publish on failure). Add global edge checks. This is the primary data quality safeguard — it ensures users never see corrupt or incomplete data. QC failure keeps the previous valid run as current.

title: QC gate blocks publish on failure
description: Modify the publish workflow: QC must pass before the publish step executes. On QC failure: run transitions to failed; previous valid run remains current; alert fires. No manual override without explicit ops action (separate force-publish command with audit trail). This is the primary data integrity safeguard.

title: Global edge checks
description: Add checks for: anti-meridian continuity (no seam artifacts at ±180°), polar clipping (data valid to expected latitude bounds), coastline mask alignment (land/sea mask consistent with source model). These checks catch spatial data corruption that per-variable range checks would miss.

title: Provenance recording in QC results
description: Store QC pass/fail results per check category in the STAC item's provenance fields. This enables audit queries: did this run pass all QC checks? Which ones failed? Include check name, status, threshold, actual value, and timestamp for each QC check executed.

title: Full OpenTelemetry Tracing
description: Extend Phase 1's basic metrics/logging to full distributed tracing. A single trace should follow a request from ingestion trigger → pipeline steps → tile generation → API response → frontend rendering. This is essential for debugging latency issues and understanding the system end-to-end.

title: Distributed tracing (ingest → tile → UI)
description: Propagate trace context (W3C Trace Context) across all service boundaries. Argo workflow steps pass trace IDs. Tile requests carry trace context. Create parent spans for pipeline runs, child spans for each step. A single trace should follow a request from ingestion trigger through pipeline steps, tile generation, API response, to frontend rendering.

title: Correlation by run_id, layer, forecast_hour, request_id, tenant_id
description: Add run_id, layer, forecast_hour, request_id, and tenant_id as span attributes/tags on all traces. Enables queries like: show me all tile requests for run X layer Y, or what's the p95 latency for EDR queries by tenant Z. These dimensions are the primary axes for operational debugging and performance analysis.

title: Alerting Rules
description: Define and implement alerting rules in Prometheus/Alertmanager. These are the operational safety net — they catch problems before users notice them. Based on SLO burn rates and operational thresholds.

title: SLO burn rate alerting
description: Alert when error budget burn rate exceeds thresholds (>50% in rolling window triggers review). Cover: tile latency SLO, pipeline success SLO, availability SLO. Uses multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting pattern per Google SRE best practices. Configure in Prometheus/Alertmanager.

title: Stale data alerting
description: Alert when no new run has been published for a model within expected time window (e.g., GFS expected every 6h; alert after 8h). This catches upstream data availability issues and pipeline stalls. Per-model configurable thresholds. Include model name and last successful publish time in alert payload.

title: Pipeline failure alerting
description: Alert on any pipeline run transitioning to failed state. Include model name, run_id, failure step, and error summary in alert payload. Route to ops channel. Integrate with Alertmanager for notification routing (Slack, PagerDuty, email).

title: Storage threshold alerting
description: Alert when object storage usage exceeds 85% and 90% thresholds. Also alert on unexpected growth rate (e.g., >20% increase in 24h, which might indicate a retention GC failure). Helps prevent storage exhaustion and catches runaway data accumulation early.

title: Auth & Access Control
description: Implement authentication (OIDC/JWT), authorization (RBAC), rate limiting, and audit logging. This is the security layer required before production deployment. The tenant-ID middleware from Phase 1 is now backed by real auth tokens.

title: OIDC/JWT authentication
description: Integrate with OIDC provider (e.g., Keycloak, Auth0). Validate JWT on every API request. Extract tenant_id and user roles from JWT claims. Reject unauthenticated requests (except health endpoints). Support token refresh and expiration handling.

title: API key scoping per tenant
description: Support API keys as alternative auth for programmatic access. API keys are scoped to a specific tenant and role. Stored hashed in metadata DB. Rate limits apply per key. Key rotation support. Keys can be revoked without affecting other keys for the same tenant.

title: RBAC (viewer/operator/admin)
description: Implement role-based access.

title: Rate limiting at API gateway
description: Per-tenant + per-user rate limits. Configurable per endpoint class (tile requests get higher limits than route optimization). Return 429 with Retry-After header. Use sliding window algorithm. Store counters in Redis. Burst allowance for short spikes.

title: Audit logging
description: Log security-relevant events: admin operations, route optimizations, data exports, AIS detail queries, auth failures. Structured log format with tenant_id, user_id, action, timestamp. Append-only audit log. Queryable for compliance and incident investigation.

title: Run Catalog Retention + GC
description: Implement the retention policy and garbage collection that was stubbed in Phase 1. Configurable per model (default: keep last 7 days / 28 runs). An Argo CronWorkflow runs periodically to expire old runs and reclaim storage. Without this, storage grows unboundedly.

title: Retention policy configuration
description: Per-model retention config: max age (days) and max count (runs). Stored in metadata DB. API endpoint for admins to view/modify. Default: 7 days / 28 runs. Validation to prevent setting dangerously low values. Changes take effect on next GC cycle.

title: GC CronWorkflow
description: Argo CronWorkflow (daily): identify runs past retention, transition to expired, delete S3 prefixes (runs/<run_id>/), compact catalog index (remove expired entries). Idempotent — safe to re-run. Emit metrics on reclaimed storage (bytes freed, runs expired). Dry-run mode for verification.

title: Graceful Degradation
description: Implement the formal degradation hierarchy from the plan. Every subsystem has a defined fallback path. The system prefers stale/reduced data over outage. This is a key reliability principle: graceful degradation over hard failure.

title: TiTiler fallback to pre-rendered static tiles
description: When TiTiler is down/overloaded: serve pre-rendered static tiles for low zoom + hot AOIs (major shipping lanes). Requires pre-render step in pipeline (low zoom only). Store static tiles at a known path pattern that the fallback can resolve. Health check determines when to activate fallback.

title: EDR fallback to cached results
description: Cache recent EDR query results. When Zarr is unreachable: serve cached results with stale indicator. For routing: fall back to last-known routing cost grid. Cache keyed by query parameters + run_id. TTL matches run publish cycle.

title: AIS fallback to last-generated MVT
description: When DuckDB is unreachable: continue serving last-generated MVT tiles (they are static per AIS date anyway). Only dynamic queries (/v1/ais/vessels?bbox=...) are affected — these return 503 with explanation. MVT tile cache serves as automatic fallback.

title: X-Degraded response header
description: All services set X-Degraded: true header when serving from fallback path. Include X-Degraded-Reason for specifics (e.g., titiler-unavailable, zarr-unreachable, ais-stale). This signals the frontend to show appropriate UI indicators. Standardized across all services.

title: Frontend degraded-mode banners
description: When frontend detects X-Degraded header: show appropriate banner (Limited zoom levels available, AIS data as of [date], etc.). Data age indicator turns amber/red. Banners are non-blocking but persistent while degradation continues. Auto-clear when service recovers.

title: Weather Routing Service
description: THE core differentiator. An isochrone-based weather route optimization engine that transforms the platform from passive visualization into active decision support. Given departure/arrival points, vessel constraints, and weather forecasts, it computes the optimal route minimizing transit time while respecting safety thresholds. This is what maritime users will pay for.

title: Routing service scaffold (FastAPI)
description: New FastAPI service: POST /v1/routes/optimize. Input validation (departure, arrival, vessel profile, constraints). Health endpoints. OpenTelemetry instrumented. Horizontally scalable (stateless — all state in request + Zarr). Dockerfile and Kubernetes deployment manifest. Structured logging.

title: Isochrone solver algorithm
description: Core algorithm: at each time step, expand wavefront of reachable positions from departure. At each grid point, evaluate vessel speed accounting for weather conditions. Prune dominated positions (another position is farther along the route and reached at same/earlier time). After reaching destination, backtrack optimal path. Literature reference: Hagiwara (1989). Grid resolution matches weather model (0.25 deg for GFS).

title: Zarr field sampling for routing
description: Read wind speed/direction, wave height/period/direction, and currents (when available) from the current published Zarr store. Use xarray for efficient point sampling at isochrone positions. Cache opened Zarr datasets in memory. Handle missing variables gracefully.

title: Routing cost grid computation + caching
description: Pre-compute routing cost grids from Zarr: for each grid point and time step, compute effective vessel speed given weather conditions. Cache as numpy arrays per run. Invalidate on new run publish (listen to SSE run.published event internally). Reduces per-request Zarr I/O significantly.

title: Anti-meridian handling in isochrone expansion
description: Handle isochrone wavefront crossing plus/minus 180 deg longitude. Split expansion at the anti-meridian and reconnect. Essential for transpacific routes. Ensure distance calculations and bearing computations work correctly across the date line.

title: POST /v1/routes/optimize API contract
description: Full API implementation: input (departure with time, arrival, vessel profile with speed/constraints, model selection, options). Output: route_id, waypoints with per-leg ETA/weather/risk, total transit hours, great-circle comparison, savings, warnings. OpenAPI documented. Pydantic request/response models.

title: Route result schema
description: Define and document the route result JSON schema. Per-waypoint: lat, lon, ETA, wind_kts, wave_m, current risk level. Summary: total transit hours, great-circle comparison, savings percentage. Warnings for segments approaching thresholds. GeoJSON-compatible geometry for map rendering.

title: Frontend - Route Planner
description: Frontend UI for route planning: draw routes manually on the map, request optimized routes from the routing service, and visualize weather along the track with risk hotspots.

title: Draw route manually on map
description: MapLibre draw interaction: click to add waypoints, drag to adjust, delete waypoints. Render as a line layer. Waypoints are sent to routing/EDR services. Snap-to-water option to prevent land-crossing waypoints. Show waypoint coordinates and leg distances.

title: Request optimized route from routing service
description: UI to set departure time, vessel profile (speed, wave/wind thresholds), and request optimization. Show loading state. Display returned optimized route overlaid on map alongside the manual/great-circle route for comparison. Show transit time savings prominently.

title: Display route with weather along track
description: Chart panel showing weather conditions along the route over time: wind speed, wave height, pressure. X-axis is route distance or time, Y-axis is weather parameter. Highlight segments exceeding thresholds. Interactive: hover on chart highlights corresponding map position and vice versa.

title: Risk hotspot visualization
description: Highlight route segments where weather conditions approach or exceed vessel thresholds. Color-code the route line (green/amber/red). Show warning icons at specific risk points. Tooltip with details: which parameter is at risk, current value vs threshold, time window of exposure.

title: Phase 1: Foundations + Observability Baseline
description:
## Phase 1 — Foundations + Observability Baseline (Weeks 1–4)

**Strategic Goal:** Stand up the core data pipeline end-to-end: ingest one weather model (GFS), process to COGs, serve tiles dynamically via TiTiler, and display on a MapLibre frontend. Establish the run-centric data model, atomic publish mechanism, and observability from day one.

**Why this is first:** Everything else builds on the storage layout, run lifecycle, and tile serving path. Without this foundation, no subsequent phase can deliver visible value. Observability is included here (not deferred) because debugging a pipeline without metrics/logs is orders of magnitude harder — we pay the instrumentation cost once, early.

**Key architectural decisions baked in here:**
- S3-compatible object storage layout (immutable run paths)
- Run catalog index (append-only, replaces naive latest.json)
- Atomic publish (staged → validated → published, never partial runs visible)
- TiTiler for dynamic tiling (not pre-rendered tile pyramids)
- OpenTelemetry from the start
- Tenant-ID middleware plumbed through even for single-tenant MVP

**Acceptance criteria:**
- New run appears only after publish flip
- Tiles load smoothly at multiple zoom levels
- Grafana dashboard shows tile latency and pipeline status
- Health probes functional in Kubernetes
close_reason: Closed

title: Object Storage Layout & Run Catalog
description: Establish the S3-compatible object storage directory structure and the append-only run catalog index that is the foundation of the run-centric data model. Everything in the platform references runs by their path in this layout. WHY: Without a well-defined storage contract, every service would need to independently resolve data paths, leading to inconsistency and brittleness. WHAT: Defines the canonical bucket layout (models/runs/staging), the run ID format, the catalog index schema, and retention policy configuration. This feature is the bedrock that all other pipeline features build upon.
notes: Key design decisions: run IDs are YYYYMMDDThhZ for lexicographic sorting; catalog.json is append-only for auditability; staging area is separate from published runs to enable atomic publish.
close_reason: Closed

title: S3 bucket structure + run ID format
description: Define and implement the s3://<bucket>/models/<model>/runs/<run_id>/ layout with staging area. Run ID format: YYYYMMDDThhZ. WHY: This is the physical contract that every other service depends on. Without a canonical path layout, services would need per-model path logic, leading to bugs and inconsistency. WHAT: Define the bucket structure (models/<model>/runs/<run_id>/ for published, staging/<run_id>/ for in-progress), implement path construction utilities, validate run ID format (YYYYMMDDThhZ ensures lexicographic ordering = chronological ordering). KEY CONSIDERATIONS: The staging area is separate so that partial writes are never visible to consumers. Run IDs must be deterministic from the model cycle time.
notes: Run ID format YYYYMMDDThhZ gives lexicographic = chronological ordering. Implement as a shared utility module. Include validation for run ID format. Example: 20260306T12Z for the 12Z run on March 6, 2026.
close_reason: Implemented S3 storage layout (StorageLayout, RunID, StorageConfig) with 30 passing tests. Paths: src/weatherman/storage/{paths.py,config.py}, tests/test_storage_paths.py

title: Run catalog index schema + implementation
description: Implement catalog.json as an append-only index of published runs per model. WHY: This replaces a naive latest.json single-pointer approach — it gives us history, rollback capability, and retention tracking. A single pointer tells you nothing about what happened before or what can be rolled back to. WHAT: Schema includes current_run_id, array of run entries with status/timestamps/asset pointers. Append-only semantics: new entries are added, never modified (except status transitions). KEY CONSIDERATIONS: The catalog must be consistent with the actual storage state. Include schema version field for future evolution. Design with retention awareness — entries need enough metadata for GC to make decisions.
notes: Schema fields: schema_version, model, current_run_id, runs[] with {run_id, status, published_at, superseded_at, expired_at, asset_manifest_path, processing_version}. Append-only: never delete entries, only update status. Use atomic write (write-tmp + rename) to prevent corruption.
close_reason: Implemented RunCatalog with append-only semantics, status lifecycle (published→superseded→expired), rollback, atomic file I/O, and JSON serialization. 33 passing tests. Files: src/weatherman/storage/catalog.py, tests/test_run_catalog.py

title: Retention policy stub
description: Create configurable retention policy definition (default: 7 days / 28 runs) with placeholder for GC implementation (actual GC is Phase 4). WHY: We define the config shape now so the catalog index is designed with retention in mind from day one. If we add retention after the fact, we may find the catalog lacks necessary metadata (e.g., timestamps, status fields). WHAT: Define retention policy configuration schema (max_age_days, max_runs, per_model overrides). Implement policy evaluation logic (which runs would be expired) but NOT the actual deletion. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Retention policy must be per-model configurable. The stub validates that our catalog entries contain all fields needed for future GC.
notes: Default: 7 days / 28 runs (whichever is more conservative). Config format: YAML or TOML. Include dry-run mode that logs what would be expired. Actual GC (file deletion, catalog cleanup) deferred to Phase 4.
close_reason: Implemented RetentionRule, RetentionPolicy (with per-model overrides, from_dict/to_dict serialization), evaluate_retention (read-only evaluation), and dry_run_retention (log-friendly summary). 19 passing tests. Files: src/weatherman/storage/retention.py, tests/test_retention.py

title: Tenant-ID Middleware
description: Plumb tenant_id through all API requests from day one, even though we start with a single tenant. WHY: Multi-tenancy retrofits are notoriously painful and error-prone. Adding tenant_id to every query path, cache key, and log entry after the fact requires touching every service and risks data leakage bugs. By plumbing it now (with a hardcoded default), the architecture is multi-tenant-ready from the start. WHAT: FastAPI middleware extracts tenant_id from JWT claims or API key scope, injects into downstream queries, cache keys, and logging context. Tenant-scoped tables include tenant_id column. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: Prepares for Phase 5 multi-tenancy without incurring the full cost now — just the plumbing.
notes: For MVP: default to single hardcoded tenant_id. Shared data (weather tiles) should NOT include tenant_id in cache key. Tenant-specific data (preferences, saved routes) should. Use FastAPI dependency injection for clean propagation.
close_reason: Closed

title: Extract tenant_id from request context
description: FastAPI middleware that extracts tenant_id from JWT claims or API key scope. WHY: Plumbing tenant_id through the system now avoids a painful retrofit when multi-tenancy is needed in Phase 5. Adding tenant isolation after the fact requires touching every service, every query, and every cache key — high risk of data leakage bugs. WHAT: FastAPI middleware that extracts tenant_id from JWT claims (preferred) or API key scope. For MVP: default to a single hardcoded tenant. The important thing is the plumbing exists — every request has a tenant_id in its context. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Must work with both authenticated and unauthenticated requests (MVP). Must be easy to swap from hardcoded to real extraction later.
notes: FastAPI dependency: async def get_tenant_id(request: Request) -> str. For MVP: return 'default'. Later: extract from JWT sub/tenant claim or API key lookup. Store in request.state.tenant_id and in structlog context. All downstream functions accept tenant_id parameter.
close_reason: Closed

title: Inject tenant_id into query paths
description: Pass tenant_id through to all database queries, cache keys, and logging context. WHY: If tenant_id is extracted but not propagated, the plumbing is incomplete and the Phase 5 retrofit is still painful. Every data access path must be tenant-aware from the start. WHAT: Tenant-scoped database tables include tenant_id column with index. Cache keys for tenant-specific data include tenant_id prefix. Logging context includes tenant_id in every log entry. Shared data (weather tiles) does NOT include tenant_id in cache key (weather data is shared across tenants). KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Must clearly distinguish tenant-scoped vs shared data. SQL queries for tenant data must always include WHERE tenant_id = ? — never allow cross-tenant queries.
notes: Tenant-scoped: user preferences, saved routes, API keys, usage tracking. Shared: weather runs, COGs, tiles, catalog. DB: add tenant_id column + index to tenant-scoped tables. Cache keys: 'tenant:{tenant_id}:preferences' vs 'tiles:{run_id}:{z}:{x}:{y}'. Use SQLAlchemy query filter or repository pattern to enforce tenant_id in queries.

title: GFS Ingestion Pipeline

WHY: The platform has no value without fresh weather data flowing in. GFS is the foundational global model. WHAT: Herbie abstracts source selection (NOMADS vs AWS mirrors) and handles retries/paths. Implements both poll-based (cron) and event-driven (SNS) triggers. Includes reliability controls (DLQ, retry, replay) to ensure no data is silently lost. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: Every downstream feature (COG processing, tile serving, frontend display) depends on data entering the system through this pipeline.

title: Herbie-based GFS downloader
description: Implement the ingestion job that uses Herbie to download GFS GRIB2 files for required variables/hours. WHY: Herbie abstracts source selection (NOMADS vs AWS mirrors) and handles retries/paths, saving us from maintaining fragile URL construction logic. WHAT: Poll-based trigger initially (cron schedule checking for new cycles). Download GRIB2 for required variables (temperature, U-wind, V-wind) and forecast hours. Stage raw GRIB2 into staging/<run_id>/ in object storage. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: NOMADS has rate limits; Herbie's source fallback helps. Must handle partial availability (not all forecast hours available simultaneously). Log download progress for observability.
