close_reason: Herbie-based GFS downloader with variable subsetting, partial-failure resilience, cycle availability checks. 11 tests all passing.

title: Cloud mirror / SNS trigger option
description: Add event-driven trigger path: subscribe SQS to AWS NOAA SNS topic for GFS, trigger ingestion on new data notification. WHY: This eliminates polling delay — instead of checking every 15 minutes, we react to data the moment it's available. Reduces latency from data availability to ingestion start by up to 15 minutes. WHAT: Subscribe SQS queue to AWS NOAA SNS topic for GFS. Lambda or worker polls SQS and triggers ingestion job. Implement as alternative trigger, not replacement for polling. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Requires AWS infrastructure (SQS, IAM). SNS topic format may change. Must handle duplicate notifications idempotently.
notes: AWS NOAA SNS topic ARN for GFS: check NOAA Big Data Program docs. SQS visibility timeout should exceed max ingestion time. Implement as a trigger adapter — same downstream pipeline, different trigger source. Keep poll-based trigger as fallback.
close_reason: Implemented SQS/SNS trigger adapter with TriggerSource protocol, PollingTrigger, SQSTrigger, and NOAA notification parser. 21 tests all passing.

title: Ingestion reliability controls
description: Durable queue with DLQ for failed events, exponential backoff + jitter retry, persist failed payloads for replay. WHY: Weather data ingestion failures must never be silent — a missed run means stale data served to users making safety-critical decisions. WHAT: Implement durable queue for ingestion events, dead-letter queue for failures after max retries, exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering herd on source recovery. Include replay_ingest_events CLI tool for targeted backfills. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Failed payloads must be persisted with full context (what was being downloaded, which attempt, error details). Replay tool must be idempotent.
notes: Use exponential backoff with jitter: base=1s, max=300s, jitter=±50%. DLQ retention: 14 days minimum. replay_ingest_events CLI: accept run_id or date range, support dry-run mode. Log all retry attempts with attempt number and error.
close_reason: Implemented RetryPolicy (exponential backoff + jitter, base=1s, max=300s, ±50%), IngestEvent (full context with error history), IngestQueue (file-backed durable queue with pending/in_progress/completed/dlq dirs, atomic writes, retry scheduling, DLQ replay). 25 passing tests. Files: src/weatherman/ingest/retry.py, src/weatherman/ingest/queue.py, tests/test_ingest_reliability.py

title: COG Processing (Initial Layers)
description: Convert GRIB2 data to Cloud Optimized GeoTIFFs for the first two layers (temperature, wind speed). WHY: COGs are the map-optimized raster format that TiTiler serves tiles from. Without COGs, we cannot serve dynamic map tiles. WHAT: Extracts weather variables from staged GRIB2 files, writes COGs with internal overviews (2x-16x) and deflate compression. Internal overviews enable fast low-zoom reads without separate pyramid generation. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: This is the data transformation step between raw model output and serveable map tiles. The COG format is central to the 'cloud-native rasters, not tile explosions' design principle.
notes: Use rasterio/GDAL for COG creation. Wind speed requires U/V component vector math. Overview factors directly impact tile latency — benchmark carefully. Output path: runs/<run_id>/cogs/<layer>/<forecast_hour>.tif

title: GRIB2 → COG pipeline for temperature

WHY: Temperature is the first layer to implement because it's a straightforward single-variable extraction — good for proving the pipeline before tackling derived variables. WHAT: Read TMP variable from GRIB2, reproject if needed, write as Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF with internal overviews and deflate compression. Output to runs/<run_id>/cogs/temperature/<forecast_hour>.tif. Validate CRS (EPSG:4326) and global extent. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: COG creation must be deterministic for the same input. Overview levels directly affect file size and tile serving performance.
notes: Use rasterio with GDAL COG driver. Deflate compression offers good size/speed tradeoff. Validate output with rio cogeo validate. Overview resampling: 'average' for continuous data like temperature. Output CRS: EPSG:4326. This pipeline code will be reused for wind speed task.

title: GRIB2 → COG pipeline for wind speed
description: Same pipeline as temperature but for wind speed (computed from U/V components in GRIB2). WHY: Wind is the most-used maritime weather variable — vessel routing, port operations, and safety decisions all depend on wind data. This is the second layer and validates the pipeline handles derived variables. WHAT: Extract UGRD and VGRD from GRIB2, compute wind speed as sqrt(u² + v²), write as COG with same parameters as temperature pipeline. Document the U/V → speed conversion clearly. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: U/V components must come from the same forecast hour and pressure level. Wind speed is always non-negative. Consider also generating wind direction as a future layer.
notes: Wind speed = sqrt(UGRD² + VGRD²). Use numpy for vectorized computation. Same COG parameters as temperature (deflate, internal overviews 2x/4x/8x/16x). Output path: runs/<run_id>/cogs/wind_speed/<forecast_hour>.tif. Reuse pipeline infrastructure from temperature task.

title: COG internal overview generation
description: Ensure all COGs have appropriate internal overviews for the zoom range we need (global to ~z10). WHY: Internal overviews directly impact tile latency at low zoom levels. Without overviews, TiTiler must read and downsample full-resolution data for every low-zoom tile request, which is orders of magnitude slower. WHAT: Benchmark overview factor vs file size tradeoff (2x/4x/8x/16x vs 2x/4x/8x/16x/32x). Validate that overviews cover the zoom range from z0 (global) to z10 (regional). Create shared configuration for overview parameters. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: More overview levels = larger files but faster low-zoom tile serving. Find the sweet spot for our use case.

title: Atomic Publish Mechanism
description: Implement the core invariant of the platform: users NEVER see partial runs. WHY: Partial data visibility is the #1 correctness risk in a weather data platform. If a user sees half-processed data, they may make dangerous decisions (e.g., routing a vessel through a storm that hasn't rendered yet). WHAT: A run transitions through lifecycle states (discovered → ingesting → staged → validated → published → superseded → expired), and only the atomic publish flip makes data visible. This is the single most important correctness property of the system. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: Every consumer of weather data (tile server, API, frontend) relies on this mechanism to guarantee data completeness.
notes: Idempotency key: model + run_id + processing_version. SQLite for dev, PostgreSQL for prod. Advisory locks prevent concurrent publish corruption. Staging artifacts preserved on failure for debugging.
close_reason: All 3 children complete: wx-ir5.4.1 (lifecycle state machine, 18 tests), wx-ir5.4.2 (staging→publish+catalog, 17 tests), wx-ir5.4.3 (per-model lock, 9 tests). Full suite 217 tests passing. Fixed minor docstring typo (4-step→5-step).

title: Run lifecycle state machine
description: Implement state transitions with validation (no skipping states, no backward transitions except rollback). WHY: The state machine enforces the invariant that data progresses through a defined lifecycle, preventing invalid states like 'published but not validated'. Without enforcement, bugs in any pipeline step could expose incomplete data. WHAT: States: discovered → ingesting → staged → validated → published → superseded → expired. Enforce valid transitions only. Idempotency key: model + run_id + processing_version. Store in metadata DB (SQLite for dev, PostgreSQL for prod). KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Rollback is the only backward transition (published → staged). State transitions must be atomic and logged for audit.
notes: Use an enum for states and a transition table for valid transitions. Include timestamp for each transition. Idempotency key prevents duplicate processing. Consider using SQLAlchemy for DB abstraction (SQLite dev / PostgreSQL prod). Log every state transition with context.
close_reason: Closed

title: Staging → publish artifact move + catalog update
description: On validation success: move artifacts from staging/<run_id>/ to runs/<run_id>/, append to catalog index, update current_run_id — all in one transaction. WHY: This is the atomic publish operation that ensures users never see partial data. If any step fails, the entire publish must not take effect. WHAT: Move COGs and metadata from staging to published path. Append run entry to catalog.json. Update current_run_id pointer. All in one logical transaction. On failure: staging artifacts remain for debugging, catalog is not updated. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Object storage doesn't have transactions — must use a sequence of operations with rollback capability. The catalog update is the commit point.
notes: Sequence: 1) Copy artifacts staging→published, 2) Verify all artifacts copied, 3) Update catalog.json (atomic write), 4) Delete staging copy. If step 3 fails, published artifacts are orphaned but harmless (no catalog entry). If step 4 fails, staging copy remains (cleanup later). The catalog write is the linearization point.
close_reason: Closed

title: Per-model publish lock
description: Acquire advisory lock before catalog updates to prevent concurrent publishes for the same model from corrupting the catalog. WHY: Two pipeline runs for the same model could race to update the catalog simultaneously, resulting in a corrupted or inconsistent catalog index. This is a concurrency safety mechanism. WHAT: Implement advisory locking per model before catalog update. Options: file-based lock for single-node, distributed lock (Redis/PostgreSQL advisory locks) for multi-node. Lock scope: per-model (different models can publish concurrently). KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Lock must have a timeout to prevent deadlocks from crashed processes. Lock granularity is per-model to maximize concurrency.
notes: For dev/single-node: file-based lock (fcntl.flock or filelock library). For prod/multi-node: PostgreSQL advisory locks (pg_advisory_lock with model hash). Lock timeout: 60 seconds. Always release lock in finally block. Log lock acquisition/release for debugging contention.
close_reason: Implemented FilePublishLock with fcntl.flock (non-blocking + timeout retry), NullPublishLock for testing, integrated into publish_run as optional lock= parameter. 9 tests covering serialization, timeout, exception safety, model independence, and backward compatibility.

Pre-generating tile pyramids would require millions of tiles per run; TiTiler renders on demand from COGs. WHAT: Deploy TiTiler (Docker), configure S3 access, expose XYZ/OGC tile endpoints with run_id-versioned URLs, and set immutable caching headers on published tiles. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: This is the bridge between stored COG data and the frontend map. Versioned URLs + immutable caching means zero cache invalidation complexity.

title: XYZ / OGC Tiles API endpoint
description: Expose tile endpoint supporting both standard XYZ (/{z}/{x}/{y}.png) and OGC Tiles API patterns. WHY: XYZ is the de facto standard for web map libraries (MapLibre, Leaflet). OGC Tiles is the emerging standard for interoperability. Supporting both maximizes client compatibility. WHAT: URL includes run_id for versioned, immutable tile paths: /tiles/{model}/{run_id}/{layer}/{forecast_hour}/{z}/{x}/{y}.png. This URL structure is fundamental — changing it later would break all clients. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Run ID in the URL means each run's tiles are at unique URLs, enabling aggressive caching. Must handle 'latest' alias that resolves to current published run_id.

Also support /tiles/{model}/latest/{layer}/... that resolves current_run_id from catalog. Consider adding /tiles/{model}/{run_id}/tilejson.json for MapLibre integration.

title: Immutable caching headers for published tiles
description: Set Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable on tiles for published runs (they never change). WHY: This is the foundation of the caching strategy. Because run_id is in the URL, tiles for a published run are permanently immutable — the same URL always returns the same tile. This means zero cache invalidation complexity. CDN, browser, and intermediate caches all benefit. WHAT: Add middleware that checks if the requested run is published (vs latest alias). For published run tiles: set immutable headers. For 'latest' alias: set short TTL or no-cache. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Only published run tiles get immutable headers. 'latest' alias must not be cached long or users see stale data after new publishes.
notes: Headers for published tiles: Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable. Headers for 'latest' alias: Cache-Control: public, max-age=60 (or use ETag/If-None-Match). This one middleware decision eliminates all cache invalidation complexity from the architecture.
close_reason: Closed

title: Health Endpoints
description: Every service exposes /health/live and /health/ready with dependency-aware readiness checks. WHY: This is table-stakes for production Kubernetes deployment. Without health endpoints, Kubernetes cannot route traffic intelligently — unhealthy pods receive requests, and rolling deployments cannot verify new pods are ready. WHAT: Liveness checks (process running, event loop responsive) and readiness checks (dependency-aware: TiTiler reachable, DB connected, published run available). Includes Kubernetes probe configuration with appropriate timeouts. HOW IT SERVES THE PROJECT: Ensures zero-downtime deployments and automatic recovery from transient failures.
notes: Liveness: simple 200 OK, check every 10s, fail after 3. Readiness: per-service dependency checks. Include degraded-but-serving states (e.g., tile service with stale data is degraded but still serving). Define probes in Helm charts.
close_reason: Closed

title: /health/live endpoint
description: Simple liveness check: process running, event loop responsive. WHY: Kubernetes liveness probes detect hung processes and restart them automatically. Without liveness checks, a deadlocked service continues receiving traffic but never responds, causing cascading failures. WHAT: Returns 200 OK with minimal JSON body {status: 'alive'}. No dependency checks — this only verifies the process is running and the event loop is responsive. Kubernetes liveness probe target: check every 10s, fail after 3 consecutive failures, restart pod. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Must be extremely fast (no I/O, no DB queries). Must never false-positive (returning 500 when the service is actually healthy).
notes: Implementation: simple FastAPI endpoint returning {status: 'alive', timestamp: now()}. No database calls, no external service checks. Response time target: <5ms. Kubernetes probe config: periodSeconds: 10, failureThreshold: 3, timeoutSeconds: 2. Put on a separate port if main port might be overwhelmed.
close_reason: Closed

title: /health/ready endpoint (dependency-aware)
description: Readiness check per service: verify downstream dependencies are reachable. WHY: Kubernetes readiness probes prevent traffic from reaching pods that cannot serve requests properly. A tile service that cannot reach TiTiler or S3 should not receive tile requests — they would all fail. WHAT: Per-service readiness checks: Tile Service checks TiTiler reachable + at least one published run available; Catalog Service checks metadata DB connected; Ingestion Service checks object storage writable. Return 200 when ready, 503 when not. Include degraded-but-serving states. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Readiness checks must have reasonable timeouts (don't block on slow dependencies). Include details of what passed/failed in response body for debugging.
notes: Response body: {status: 'ready'|'degraded'|'not_ready', checks: {db: {status, latency_ms}, s3: {status, latency_ms}, titiler: {status, latency_ms}}}. Degraded: some non-critical dependencies down but core function works. Timeouts: 2s per check, 5s total. Cache check results for 5s to avoid hammering dependencies.
close_reason: Closed

title: Kubernetes probe configuration
description: Define liveness and readiness probes in Kubernetes manifests/Helm charts. WHY: Probes must be configured with appropriate timeouts and failure thresholds per service — too aggressive and pods restart unnecessarily during load spikes, too lenient and unhealthy pods serve errors for too long. WHAT: Configure probes in Helm chart values for each service. Liveness: httpGet /health/live, periodSeconds 10, failureThreshold 3. Readiness: httpGet /health/ready, periodSeconds 5, failureThreshold 2. Startup probes for slow-starting services. KEY CONSIDERATIONS: Different services have different startup times and failure characteristics. Pipeline services may need longer timeouts during heavy processing.
notes: Helm values per service. Tile service: readiness failureThreshold 2 (fast detection). Pipeline workers: startup probe with 120s timeout (COG processing is slow). Use initialDelaySeconds for services that need warm-up. Document rationale for each threshold in Helm chart comments.

title: App factory with lifespan and middleware wiring
description:
Create the core create_app() factory function in src/weatherman/app.py. This is the central composition point that wires all Phase 1 modules into a runnable FastAPI application.

WHY THIS IS THE CRITICAL PATH: Every other task in this feature either feeds into or depends on the app factory. It is the single function that transforms a collection of library modules into a running server. Without it, nothing works end-to-end.

WHAT create_app() DOES:

1. CONFIGURATION — Read from env vars with sensible defaults:
   - WEATHERMAN_DATA_DIR → LocalObjectStore for dev (no S3 needed)
   - STORAGE_BUCKET, STORAGE_ENDPOINT_URL, STORAGE_REGION, STORAGE_PREFIX → StorageConfig
   - TITILER_BASE_URL → default http://localhost:8080
   - CORS_ORIGINS → default http://localhost:5173

2. STORAGE BACKEND — Create ObjectStore instance:
   - If WEATHERMAN_DATA_DIR set: LocalObjectStore(Path(data_dir))
   - Otherwise: raise clear error (S3ObjectStore not yet implemented — Phase 2 concern)
   - Create catalog_loader callable shared between catalog API routes and TileService

3. LIFESPAN (async context manager):
   Startup:
     - setup_logging(service_name="weatherman") — structlog JSON output
     - setup_tracing(service_name="weatherman") — OTel TracerProvider + auto-instrumentation
     - init_tile_service(storage_config, titiler_base_url, catalog_loader) — TileService singleton
     - register_check(TiTilerHealthCheck(titiler_base_url)) — readiness dependency
   Shutdown:
     - await shutdown_tile_service() — close httpx client
     - shutdown_tracing() — flush OTel spans
     - clear_checks() — reset health check registry

4. MIDDLEWARE (order is critical — listed outermost to innermost):
   a. CORSMiddleware — handles preflight OPTIONS, must be outermost so CORS headers appear on all responses including error responses
   b. PrometheusMiddleware — records request latency/count/active connections for all endpoints
   c. TenantMiddleware — extracts tenant_id, binds to request.state and structlog context

5. ROUTER MOUNTING:
   - app.include_router(api_router) — /api/catalog/{model}, /api/manifest/{model}/{run_id}
   - app.include_router(health_router) — /health/live, /health/ready
   - app.include_router(tile_router) — /tiles/{model}/{run_id}/{layer}/{fh}/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
   - app.add_route("/metrics", metrics_endpoint, methods=["GET"])

FACTORY SIGNATURE:
  def create_app(*, data_dir: str | None = None, titiler_base_url: str | None = None) -> FastAPI

  Parameters allow test overrides without env vars. Production uses env vars; tests pass tmp_path and a dummy TiTiler URL.

FORWARD COMPATIBILITY (how Phases 2-4 extend this):
- Phase 2 (EDR/SSE/AIS): app.include_router(edr_router), app.include_router(ais_router)
- Phase 3 (AIS queries): app.include_router(ais_query_router)
- Phase 4 (Routing + Auth): app.include_router(routing_router, prefix="/v1"), add auth middleware after CORS
- Each new router is a single include_router() call — no factory refactoring needed

- weatherman.health: router, register_check, clear_checks
- weatherman.tenancy: TenantMiddleware
- weatherman.observability: setup_logging, setup_tracing, shutdown_tracing, PrometheusMiddleware, metrics_endpoint

title: Catalog & manifest API routes
description:
Create the two GET endpoints the frontend needs to discover and configure weather data layers. These are the API surface that bridges the storage layer (RunCatalog, UIManifest) to the frontend hooks (useDataAge, useManifest).

WHY THESE ENDPOINTS EXIST:
The frontend operates in a pull model: it polls the catalog to discover the current run, fetches the manifest to learn what layers/forecast hours are available, then constructs tile URLs. Without these endpoints, the frontend has no way to know what data exists or how to request it. The catalog endpoint is polled every 5 minutes (configurable) by the useDataAge hook; the manifest endpoint is fetched once per run change by the useManifest hook.

ENDPOINT 1: GET /api/catalog/{model}
- Loads {StorageLayout(model).catalog_path} via ObjectStore (e.g., models/gfs/catalog.json)
- Parses with RunCatalog.from_json(), returns catalog.to_dict()
- Frontend expects: { model, current_run_id, runs: [{ run_id, status, published_at }] }
- Returns 404 if catalog file not found (model doesn't exist or no data ingested yet)

ENDPOINT 2: GET /api/manifest/{model}/{run_id}
- Resolves manifest path via StorageLayout(model).manifest_path(RunID(run_id))
- Parses with UIManifest.from_json(), returns manifest.to_dict()
- Frontend expects: { schema_version: 1, model, run_id, cycle_time, published_at, resolution_km, layers: [...], forecast_hours: [...], tile_url_template }
- Frontend validates schema_version === 1 (throws on mismatch)
- Returns 404 if manifest file not found

DESIGN DECISIONS:
- Routes defined in an inline APIRouter(prefix="/api") within app.py — only 2 endpoints, doesn't justify a separate module yet. When Phase 2 adds EDR/SSE (5+ new endpoints), those go in their own router modules.
- The catalog_loader callable (model -> RunCatalog) is shared with TileService, which needs it for "latest" run_id resolution. This avoids loading the catalog twice.
- ObjectStore instance is created once in the app factory and shared via closure.
- Error handling: FileNotFoundError from ObjectStore.read_bytes() → HTTPException(404). ValueError from RunID/catalog parsing → HTTPException(400).

EXISTING CODE TO REUSE:
- weatherman.storage.object_store.ObjectStore / LocalObjectStore (src/weatherman/storage/object_store.py)
- weatherman.storage.paths.StorageLayout — .catalog_path, .manifest_path(run_id)
- weatherman.storage.catalog.RunCatalog — .from_json(), .to_dict()
- weatherman.storage.manifest.UIManifest — .from_json(), .to_dict()

title: FastAPI App Composition Layer
description:
Phase 1 built all backend modules as standalone libraries — tile routing (TiTiler proxy), health probes (/health/live, /health/ready), tenant-ID middleware, observability (logging, tracing, metrics), storage catalog, and UI manifest generation. However, there is NO FastAPI application that composes these modules into a runnable HTTP server.

The frontend (Vite + React + MapLibre) expects three backend API surfaces:
- GET /api/catalog/{model} — run catalog JSON (polled every 5 min by useDataAge hook)
- GET /api/manifest/{model}/{run_id} — UI manifest JSON (fetched on run change by useManifest hook)
- GET /tiles/{model}/{run_id}/{layer}/{forecast_hour}/{z}/{x}/{y}.png — raster tiles (loaded by MapLibre via useWeatherLayer hook)
- GET /health/live, /health/ready — Kubernetes probes
- GET /metrics — Prometheus scrape endpoint

All currently fail with connection refused because no server exists.

WHY THIS MATTERS: Without this composition layer, Phase 1 deliverables cannot be demonstrated end-to-end. The individual modules are tested in isolation, but there is no way to start a server, point the frontend at it, and see weather tiles on the map. This is the glue that makes Phase 1 deliverable as a running system rather than a collection of library code.

DESIGN GOALS:
- Forward-compatible with Phases 2-4: new routers added via app.include_router() without refactoring the factory
- Testable: create_app() factory accepts overrides (data_dir, titiler_base_url) so tests use temp dirs
- Dev-friendly: LocalObjectStore (already exists) for development — no S3 required. WEATHERMAN_DATA_DIR env var
- Observable from day one: Prometheus middleware, structured logging, OTel tracing all wired at startup in lifespan
- Tenant-ID plumbed through all requests (single tenant MVP, multi-tenant ready for Phase 5)

KEY ARCHITECTURAL DECISIONS:
1. App factory pattern (create_app) not module-level global — enables test isolation
2. Lifespan context manager (not deprecated on_event) for startup/shutdown
3. Catalog/manifest routes inline in app.py (only 2 endpoints) — Phase 2 adds dedicated router modules
4. ObjectStore protocol shared between catalog API and TileService for "latest" resolution
5. Middleware order: CORS (outermost) → Prometheus → Tenant (innermost)

FILES TO MODIFY: pyproject.toml (add uvicorn), frontend/vite.config.ts (add /tiles proxy)

- uv run python -m weatherman starts server on port 8000 without error
- curl localhost:8000/health/live returns {"status": "alive"}
- curl localhost:8000/metrics returns Prometheus text format
- Frontend dev server (localhost:5173) can reach /api and /tiles endpoints via proxy
- All existing tests still pass; new integration tests pass

title: Wind Particle Foundation
description: GPU-based particle system foundation for wind animation. This creates the low-level WebGL infrastructure that Phase 3's GPU Particle Engine (wx-0pg.2) builds upon: particle state textures with ping-pong rendering, particle draw pass, trail compositing with fade. This is the 'engine' — wx-0pg.2 adds the wind-field advection logic, spawn/despawn, and speed-based density.
notes:
## Architecture
Ping-Pong State Textures:
- Two 256x256 RGBA32F textures store particle positions (R,G = lon,lat) and age (B)
- Each frame: read from texture A, write updated positions to texture B, then swap
- FBO (framebuffer object) rendering for GPU-to-GPU state update
- No CPU readback — everything stays on GPU

Draw Pass:
- Vertex shader reads particle position from state texture
- Renders point sprites or short line segments (for motion blur)
- Color/opacity based on wind speed at particle location

Trail Composite:
- Render particles to offscreen texture
- Composite with previous frame at reduced opacity (0.95) for trail effect
- Final composite onto map with additive or screen blending

## Files
- frontend/src/layers/WindParticleLayer.ts (new — CustomLayerInterface)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-update.vert.glsl
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-update.frag.glsl
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-draw.vert.glsl
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-draw.frag.glsl

## Acceptance Criteria
- 65536 particles rendered at 60fps on mid-range GPU
- Smooth trails that fade over ~2 seconds
- No GPU memory leaks over extended animation periods
- Clean integration with MapLibre render loop

title: Particle state texture ping-pong
description: Implement GPU-side particle state management using ping-pong framebuffer rendering. Two 256x256 RGBA32F textures store particle positions and age. Each frame, a fullscreen quad pass reads from texture A, computes updated positions (for now, simple random walk — wind advection comes in wx-0pg.2), and writes to texture B. Then swap. All state stays on GPU — zero CPU readback.
notes:
## Architecture
State Texture Layout (256x256 = 65536 particles):
- R: longitude (normalized to [0,1] for texture storage)
- G: latitude (normalized to [0,1])
- B: age (0.0 = just spawned, 1.0 = ready to despawn)
- A: speed (for draw pass coloring, or reserved)

## Ping-Pong Flow
1. Create two RGBA32F textures: stateA, stateB
2. Create two FBOs: fboA (writes to stateA), fboB (writes to stateB)
3. Each frame:
   a. Bind fboB as render target
   b. Bind stateA as input texture
   c. Render fullscreen quad with update shader
   d. Swap: A<->B
4. Update shader (for now): position += small random offset, age += dt

## Initialization
- Fill initial state texture with random positions in viewport
- Use a seed texture or gl.texSubImage2D with Float32Array

## Files
- frontend/src/layers/WindParticleLayer.ts (new — FBO management, ping-pong loop)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-update.vert.glsl (passthrough)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-update.frag.glsl (state update logic)

## Acceptance Criteria
- 65536 particles maintain state across frames
- No CPU readback in the hot path
- Random walk particles visible and moving
- GPU memory stable (no leaks from FBO/texture creation)

title: Particle draw + trail composite
description: Render particles from state texture and composite with trail fading. The draw pass reads particle positions from the state texture and renders point sprites. The trail pass composites the current frame with the previous frame at reduced opacity (0.95) creating smooth motion trails. The final composite blends onto the map.
notes:
## Three-Pass Rendering

Pass 1: Draw Particles
- Vertex shader: read position from state texture using gl_VertexID
- Convert lon/lat to screen coordinates using MapLibre matrix
- Render as GL_POINTS with gl_PointSize based on zoom
- Fragment shader: circular point with soft edge (smoothstep)
- Color: white or wind-speed-colored

Pass 2: Trail Composite
- Render previous trail texture as fullscreen quad at opacity 0.95
- Then render current particle points on top
- Output to trail FBO
- This creates exponential decay trails

Pass 3: Map Composite
- MapLibre calls our render() — draw trail texture onto map
- Use screen blending or additive for bright-on-dark effect
- Alpha: controlled by user opacity slider

## Texture Setup
- trailTextureA, trailTextureB (ping-pong for trail accumulation)
- Same resolution as canvas (resize on window resize)

## Files
- frontend/src/layers/WindParticleLayer.ts (render pipeline)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-draw.vert.glsl (position from texture)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/particle-draw.frag.glsl (point rendering)
- frontend/src/layers/shaders/trail-composite.frag.glsl (trail fade)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Smooth trails that fade over ~2 seconds
- 60fps with 65536 particles on mid-range GPU
- Trails don't accumulate (reach steady state)
- Clean composite with map (no blending artifacts)
- Handles canvas resize without artifacts

title: TiTiler health check implementation
description:
Implement a TiTilerHealthCheck class that conforms to the DependencyChecker protocol from weatherman.health. This check is registered during app startup so the /health/ready endpoint can report whether the tile-serving backend is reachable.

WHY: Kubernetes readiness probes use /health/ready to decide whether to route traffic to a pod. If TiTiler is down, the tile endpoints will return 504 Gateway Timeout — but without this health check, Kubernetes would still route traffic to the pod because /health/ready would report "ready". By registering TiTiler as a critical dependency, the readiness probe returns 503 when TiTiler is unreachable, causing Kubernetes to stop routing new requests until the dependency recovers.

WHAT TO IMPLEMENT:
class TiTilerHealthCheck:
    name = "titiler"
    critical = True  # failure → not_ready (503), not just degraded

    def __init__(self, base_url: str): ...

    async def check(self) -> bool:
        # HTTP GET to {base_url}/healthz (TiTiler's built-in health endpoint)
        # Returns True if 200, False on any error/timeout
        # Uses a short timeout (2s) to avoid blocking the readiness check

EXISTING PROTOCOL (from src/weatherman/health.py):
    class DependencyChecker(Protocol):
        @property
        def name(self) -> str: ...
        @property
        def critical(self) -> bool: ...
        async def check(self) -> bool: ...

NOTES:
- In dev without TiTiler running, the check fails and /health/ready returns 503 with {"status": "not_ready", "checks": {"titiler": {"status": "fail"}}}. This is correct behavior — the app is alive but not ready to serve tiles.
- The per-check timeout is already handled by health.py's _run_single_check (2s timeout). The health check itself should use an even shorter client timeout (~1.5s) to avoid racing.
- Use httpx.AsyncClient for the check. Create a fresh client per check (simple) or share one (optimization for later).
- The TiTiler docker-compose.titiler.yml exposes health at http://localhost:8080/api (not /healthz). Verify the actual TiTiler health endpoint path.

CONSIDERATIONS:
- Future Phase 4 adds more health checks (S3 reachability, DuckDB connection). The pattern established here (small class per dependency) scales cleanly.
- Mark as critical=True. The plan doc specifies "degraded but serving" states for non-critical failures (e.g., stale data). TiTiler being down means no tiles at all, which is critical.
